basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Ḍuḥā — “The Morning Light” — is the sister of al-Sharḥ. Tradition reports that after the first revelations Gabriel ceased coming to the Prophet for some number of days — twelve, twenty-five, or forty in different versions. The Meccan opponents seized the chance to say: Muḥammad has been forsaken by his Lord. The Prophet was deeply grieved. It is then that this sūra came down, like a hand laid upon his shoulder. It opens with two oaths — the morning light, the night when it grows still — which, for Qushayrī, are the two times of the spiritual life: al-ḍuḥā is the time of witnessing (waqt al-shuhūd, وَقْت الشُّهُود), the night is the time of gathering. Then comes the overwhelming word: Your Lord has neither left you nor hated you. And the sūra, having promised the better for tomorrow (vv. 4-5), turns back to recall three graces already received — the orphan sheltered, the wanderer guided, the destitute enriched — before closing on three simple commands: do not oppress the orphan, do not rebuff the asker, proclaim the favours received. The whole sūra moves in the gesture of return: God has never ceased being present, even when the silence made one believe otherwise.

versets.commentaire: al-ḍuḥā (الضُّحَى): the early hours of the day, just after sunrise, when the light rises but is not yet at its height. For many, it also names the supererogatory prayer that bears this name — the ṣalāt al-ḍuḥā, the prayer of mid-morning. Qushayrī, more deeply, sees in al-ḍuḥā “the time of witnessing” (waqt al-shuhūd, وَقْت الشُّهُود) — the inward hour when the soul sees clearly what it is given to see. And he adds, in passing: “It is also the hour at which God spoke to Moses.” Not all hours weigh the same — some are charged with the divine word, by memory or by disposition. The morning light is not only a physical light; it is a state of the heart.

mots.def: ḍuḥā: the forenoon, the beginning of the day when the sun has risen but is not yet high. Also: the supererogatory prayer of mid-morning.

versets.trad: By the night when it grows still!
versets.commentaire: sajā (سَجَى): to grow calm, to become still, to fall silent. A very fitting image: the night is not at once peaceful — it becomes still as the sounds of day die out. Qushayrī reads the verse in several depths. First, the night of the miʿrāj (مِعْرَاج), the Prophet's nocturnal ascent. Then, more broadly, any night when God, according to the prophetic word, “descends to the lowest heaven calling: Is anyone asking that I may give him?” And still more inwardly: the night of which the Prophet said: A veil sometimes covers my heart, so I ask God's forgiveness a hundred times a day. Morning and night are thus set side by side as the two places of converse with God: the light where one sees, and the darkness where one waits.

mots.def: sajā: to grow calm, to fall silent; perfect.

versets.trad: Your Lord has neither forsaken you, nor hated you.
versets.commentaire: Here is the sentence for which the sūra came down. mā waddaʿaka (مَا وَدَّعَكَ): He has not bidden you farewell, He has not parted from you. mā qalā (مَا قَلَى): He has not hated you. Tradition reports that Gabriel had ceased coming to the Prophet for several days, and the Meccan opponents mocked him: “Muḥammad has been forsaken by his Lord.” Silence felt as a dismissal. And God sends down, after this long silence, these nine words that are the whole teaching of the sūra: He has neither left you nor hated you. The Sufis read this verse as a word addressed to every heart which, passing through a season of dryness, believes itself forsaken. God's answer: I have never ceased being here. The silence was another form of presence.

mots.def: waddaʿa: intensive form of wadaʿa; to take leave, to bid farewell. With suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: qalā: to hate, to take in aversion, to reject; perfect.

versets.trad: And surely the life to come shall be better for you than this life.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī comments: “What He will give you in the next life will be better for you than what He gives you in this one.” Then he specifies, more delicately: “the intercession (shafāʿa, شَفَاعَة), the Pool (ḥawḍ, حَوْض), and the garment of unification (libās al-tawḥīd, لِبَاس التَّوْحِيد) He will clothe you with tomorrow — that is what is better than what He gives you today.” The verse consoles doubly: not only has God not forsaken the Prophet, but what He has in store for him far exceeds what He has already given. The present silence is not the end of giving; it prepares a greater one.

mots.def: ākhira: the last, the hereafter; substantivised feminine active participle.
ūlā: the first (life), this present life.

versets.trad: The day shall come when your Lord will grant you His gifts and you will be content.
versets.commentaire: fa-tarḍā (فَتَرْضَى): “and you will be content.” Qushayrī reports a splendid exchange about this verse. The Prophet was asked: “Are you content with the gift, or with the Giver?” He answered: “No” — meaning: no, I am not content with the gift; I am content only with the Giver. This is the highest teaching. The servant's contentment (riḍā, رِضَا) does not rest on what he receives, but on the One from whom he receives. A satisfied life is one in which the heart has bound itself to the Giver rather than to the gifts — and then, whatever comes, the heart remains at peace.

mots.def: aʿṭā: to give; imperfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: raḍiya: to be content, to accept; imperfect.

versets.trad: Did He not find you an orphan and then provide a shelter?
versets.commentaire: The first of three graces recalled. Muḥammad was orphaned of his father before his birth, and lost his mother at six. He was then taken in by his grandfather ʿAbd al-Muṭṭalib, then by his uncle Abū Ṭālib. That is what the verse recalls first. But Qushayrī does not stop there. He reads: “He sheltered you in the hollow of His shade (kanaf ẓilluh, كَنَف ظِلُّه), and raised you with the delicacy of His care.” Then, deeper still: “He sheltered you on the carpet of nearness (bisāṭ al-qurba, بِسَاط القُرْبَة), where you stood alone — with no one sharing that station with you.” The lost orphan has become the received orphan: it is precisely because he had no one in the world that God took him for Himself alone.

mots.def: wajada: to find; apocopated imperfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: yatīm: orphan; primarily, one who has lost his father in childhood.
āwā: to shelter, to take in, to give refuge; perfect.

versets.trad: Did He not find you wandering and then guide you?
versets.commentaire: A delicate verse, for the word ḍāll (ضَالّ) — “wandering” — applied to the Prophet has caused much ink to flow. Qushayrī gives eight successive readings, each more inward than the last. The first, anecdotal: as a child, he had been lost in the streets of Mecca, and God guided his uncle to him. The second: you were “lost in Us” — perplexed, wondering — and We guided you from Us to Us. The third: you did not know the detail of the prescriptions, and We taught them to you. The fourth: the people around you were astray, and We guided them through you. And then these three overwhelming readings: “You were lost in Our love, and We guided you by the light of nearness toward Us.” “You were lost as regards My love for you — so I made you know that I love you.” “You did not know the rank of your nobility — so I taught you your worth.” Eight readings, but one held note: it is not moral error that is named — it is the perplexity of the lover who does not yet know that he is loved.

mots.def: wajada: to find; perfect + suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ḍāll: active participle of ḍalla, to wander, to be lost; here, perplexed, without bearing.
hadā: to guide, to lead in the straight way; perfect.

versets.trad: Did He not find you destitute and then enrich you?
versets.commentaire: ʿāʾil (عَائِل): poor, in charge of a family without means. The traditional tafsīr recalls that the Prophet was enriched by the goods of his wife Khadīja. But Qushayrī, faithful to his method, unfolds the verse: “He enriched you beyond desire and seeking (al-irāda wa-l-ṭalab, الإرَادَة وَالطَّلَب) — by making you content in loss itself (al-faqd, الفَقْد).” This is the great Sufi reading of wealth: to be rich is not to own much, it is to no longer need to own. And Qushayrī's last definition gathers it all: “He enriched you by God (aghnāka bi-Llāh, أَغْنَاكَ بِاللَّه).” The servant enriched by God Himself no longer needs anything else.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ʿāʾil: poor, supporting a family without enough means.
aghnā: to enrich, to render independent (of need); perfect.

versets.trad: The orphan, then, do not treat with harshness;
versets.commentaire: Three graces have been received (vv. 6-8); three commands follow. The first extends the first grace. taqhar (تَقْهَر) means “to oppress, to overcome by force, to crush.” Qushayrī comments: “Do not make him afraid, be gentle with him, bring him close.” Three simple words worth a treatise on education: do not frighten, treat with gentleness, draw near. All Islam's delicacy toward the orphan is in this verse. The Prophet said: “I and the guardian of an orphan will be in Paradise like these two” — and he joined his index and middle fingers. The one who has himself been an orphan has the duty of extending to another orphan the hand God extended to him.

mots.def: qahara: to oppress, to overcome by force, to crush; apocopated imperfect in prohibition.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 2nd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: the asker, then, do not turn away;
versets.commentaire: al-sāʾil (السَّائِل) means in Arabic at once “the one who asks” (the beggar) and “the one who questions” (the seeker). Both senses are intended. For the beggar: “Either you give to him,” Qushayrī says, “or you refuse him gently, with a promise of later.” The refusal itself must be delicate. And for the seeker — the one who comes searching for God without yet having found Him — Qushayrī adds: “The one who asks about Us, the one who is perplexed in Us — do not rebuff them, for it is We who guide them; and We shall unveil for them the very places of their questions. You, be gentle in speech with them.” The verse thus addresses all who are in a position to teach: do not chase away the one who seeks.

mots.def: sāʾil: one who asks (beggar) or who questions (seeker); active participle of saʾala.
nahara: to rebuff, to push away brusquely; apocopated imperfect in prohibition.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 2nd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: and your Lord's favours — proclaim them!
versets.commentaire: The last command is positive and sun-bright. fa-ḥaddith (فَحَدِّثْ): tell, say, transmit — the same root as ḥadīth, the reported word. Qushayrī sums up: “Give thanks, and declare clearly His favour upon you, His grace toward you.” The verse forbids the false modesty that would silence what God has done. When one has received, one must say so — not to boast, but to render the homage owed to the Giver. The Prophet will apply this verse all his life by repeating al-ḥamdu li-Llāh — praise be to God — at every grace received. The sūra, opened upon the silence that seemed an abandonment, closes thus on the voice that proclaims: God, for His part, has never ceased to give.

mots.def: niʿma: favour, grace; preposition bi- + noun in the construct genitive.
ḥaddatha: intensive form of ḥadatha; to tell, to transmit, to declare. Same root as ḥadīth.
